speaker: interviewer
text: --Deutsch. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Deutsch, you were telling us about being reunited with your older sister and her family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] After-- after I left that city, I went back to my hometown. I discovered that I have three more sisters that survived. And they all stayed in the house that we were raised. The house was intact. The way we left it, that's the way we found it. There were some people moved into the house. They didn't remove nothing from the house. Even found the crib that we were all 11 kids raised in that crib. That crib was still there, intact.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we stayed there for awhile. And that was before Pesach. And so, natural, Pesach, the whole families come together. But my older sister, with my-- with my brother-in-law and the three children, they stayed in the city. It's called [NON-ENGLISH]. So they were the only ones that were not in the-- the house where we were raised.

speaker: interviewee
text: Pesach came close. And I went and I sold the chametz from the house. Not that I had chametz. It was my brother's idea that we have to clean out the house. There was nothing in the house to clean out. But this is a tradition that we do. We did it. So I went and I sold the chametz to the rabbi in the city. And when it came Pesach night-- the first Pesach night-- we were all sitting at the table. That was the first time that we were all together, because my two older brothers left before there war, a couple years before, because they were in the Romanian military. So they were not-- they were not home during the time of the war. So that was the first time that we saw the two older brothers. And instead to be able to enjoy to eat something that they prepared already for Pesach, instead, we just sat at the table and we cried. We couldn't even swallow a bite, because my father and mother wasn't there. My two younger brothers wasn't there. And to see around that house after house was boarded up, nobody came home, so we were fortunate that, from the nine of us-- I mean from the 11, nine survived, because we were somehow dispersed in different areas. The oldest brother survived, because he was-- he left Romania on a ship going to Egypt. And he was on that ship coming back and forth from Romania to Egypt. He was a sailor. So that's how he survived. My older sister with the three children, my brother-in-law, they survived in the mountains. They just kept on going from-- from one place to another. They never stayed 24 hours in the same place, because they were always afraid that someone will know that there's some human beings. That's how they survived. And I survived. I was in the concentration camp. My brother, Simcha, was in the concentration camp. And my sisters-- the three sisters-- were in the camps. So we were just lucky. I think that we were about the only family of the-- of the city-- there was about 150,000 families-- that we survived. So we were fortunate. And being there, while the Russians were already there-- because the Russians occupied that part of the country after the war. They were the ones that liberated that part of the country-- the Russians, whenever they saw a young woman, they just took her and they raped her. So my wife, while we were home, was afraid to leave the house. So we decided that we are going to go back to Germany. So we had to smuggle ourselves back to Germany. Coming from Germany, the train was OK, till Austria. But from Austria, all the way to my home, we had to smuggle ourselves into the countries-- illegal, because we had no papers to show who we were. So from Austria, we smuggled ourselves to Hungary, from Hungary, to Czechoslovakia, from Czechoslovakia, to Romania. And we were there for a few weeks. [BACKGROUND NOISE] [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Continue? Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I'm trying to think. When we left home, we had to smuggle ourselves back. So we smuggled ourselves back first from Romania to Czechoslovakia and to Hungary. When we came to Budapest, we stayed there a few days. We had to find a guide that will be able to guide us from Budapest, from Hungary to Austria. So there were people that they were doing these kind of things, to smuggle people out from one country to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we discovered that there is a Russian driver that delivers-- because Budapest was also under the Russian occupancy. They were occupied by the Russians. And in Austria, they also had Russians. So the only way to deliver the post, the mail, from one place to another was trucks. So there was a truck that delivered mail from Budapest, through Austria, to Vienna. That truck driver smuggled people out. How did he smuggle people out? He had one of those half trucks, you-- the-- they're what they call here a three-quarter tons, the military truck. We were 17 people and two children underneath. And on top of us, there were sacks of mail. Because it didn't take too many hours from Budapest to Vienna, so we could hold out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we arrived to the border, and the guy just kept on-- something was going on with the papers. You know, you needed papers for the truck driver. So they kept us waiting at the border. And the two little kids started to cry. It was so hot, we were practically suffocating underneath that canvas. Top of the canvas, he put the-- the mail. So the father put his hands on the little babies, they shouldn't cry. If it would have lasted a couple more minutes, he would have suffocated those two little babies. This is how we survived coming out, trying to get back to Germany. When we arrived on the Austrian side, when the truck driver lets us off, we were caught by the Austrians, by the-- by the civil police. And so they took us. We separated, me and my wife. I didn't know my wife, my wife didn't know from me.

speaker: interviewer
text: When we arrived-- when we-- when we came off the truck in a-- in a private area where nobody knew what was going on, we were started to walk in the streets, they were-- we-- we were caught. So I was kept in the-- in the jail. I had no papers. What are they going to do with me? I had nothing on me that they could say, well, I'm a smuggler, or I'm doing something wrong.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said to them, I just came back from Romania. I'm going back to the camp where I came from. I went back home to see if anybody survived. I told them I found nothing home, so I'm coming back. So they let us go.

speaker: interviewer
text: But where do you go in the big city of Vienna? So we kept on asking questions. Same thing. There was also a place where they had-- the military had horses. It was a big stable, like a block long, like a half-- a half a acre, you know, with stables. And people, that's where they used to gather.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that's where I found my wife. So we met together. And that's where my wife conceived my daughter. [LAUGHS] It was-- it was very interesting how this happened, because we were all young couples at that time-- everybody. There were no single people, because you're all-- you were 19, 20 years old. You want to find someone. So we were married the first months in Bergen-Belsen. On the way back, that's when my daughter was conceived, in-- in Vienna. And from there, we stayed in Vienna a couple days. And we smuggled ourselves back to Bergen-Belsen. And we stayed on in Bergen-Belsen till 1949. And in '49, we came to the United States. And we are here since.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Deutsch, let me just ask you now. It's almost 50 years since the end of World War II. Is there any message that you'd like to give the world, to your children, your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] First, I want to tell you-- I have to bring up something. I was, a year ago, in Washington, DC. And my daughter got tickets to go to see the Holocaust Museum. The man that started to build the Holocaust Museum is a man by the name of Sonny Abramson. I don't know if you heard of the name. This man had given $25 million to be able to build this museum. I didn't know about this. I just discovered it. His daughter-in-law is my daughter's best friend. And the reason why they're best friends, because my daughter speaks Jewish, and she speaks Jewish. And they arranged for me to meet that Mr. Abramson. To come to see Mr. Abramson is just as hard as you want to see the President. He's one of the richest men in Washington, DC. He owns all the White Flint department stores, all the shopping centers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow, I felt, who am I to meet this man? Well, my daughter says, you must meet this man, because this man has done something that you always talked about. What was I talking about? When I was laying on the cots-- on the cot in the camps-- I was always thinking, is there anybody in the world that will know how and what we had to endure, what we had gone through to be able to survive, to tell the world what was really all-- what was this all about? So I met this man. And I was invited into a club where the President goes, all the senators, all the councilmens, everybody that's in the upper echelons, people that can only say, well, I can go there. I felt like a little bean, compared to those people. My daughter says, no, I want you to go and meet this Mr. Abramson. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were there waiting for him. It took him an hour from-- from coming in, till he came to the table. Took him an hour, because everybody stopped him. He's such a important person. I didn't know [SIGHS] if I can open my mouth and even talk to this man. The reason why we went there, because he wanted to hear from a real survivor what was it all about, that he invested $25 million, if it was worthwhile to do what he did. So he wanted to talk to someone that lived through the concentration camps. And I happened to meet this man. While we were sitting for about three hours at the table, the food was sitting there. He couldn't eat. I couldn't eat. My daughter was crying. His daughter-in-law was crying. He asked questions. And I told him, when I was 18-and-a-half years old, while I was laying on that cot thinking about it, if anybody will ever know what was going on. And here, 50 years later, I met someone that I could tell him what was it really all about, that he had established that the world should know what this concentration camps was, how people survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this was something that woke me up again to talk about it. It was very hard, till now, to really talk about the past. It's just like a wound. If you open up, you take the skin off, it starts to hurt. It takes time to heal again. It IS the same story with my life story here. It's 50 years past. Who wants to wake up and to remind and talk about it, to bring that hurt back into yourself? But the question that you asked, what do you want to tell the world? Nobody should know-- I don't wish it upon the worst enemy, anybody, any human being, to know what this was all about, how hard it is for a person to be able to survive, what a person can endure, how strong a human being is, that you can survive without food, one week, another day, another day, but for months. And people did survive to tell the world what was this horrible thing that happened. And it should not happen again, because after all, we're living in the 20th century. The world is open. There are free markets. One country is intermingled with the other country. Why should a thing like this happen? It should not happen. I wish only, for my kids and my grandchildren, they should never know what I left-- what I went through to be here to tell this. I hope this will never happen again. We should all live in peace. What can I say? What-- what-- who am I to say what it should be and how it should be? It's hard to talk about it. It's hard to tell people how to conduct life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even today's times, we have a free country, we have a free life, and listen to all the things that's going on every day. You would think already, in a country like in the United States, that has all the freedom, that you don't have to look back, there's someone behind you with a gun. And still, people are killing each other for no reason whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what voice can I say that will help to improve? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell me? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Is there anything I can say that will improve the world? I can only say what it was, and it should not happen again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if someone will say that they'll fill up this room here with billions of dollars, I should go to through again the same thing, I'd say, you keep all the money in the world. I want to have a peaceful household that I can come in, quiet, that I can enjoy my days and nights. It's very-- it's very difficult to talk about. It's very hard to tell someone what it was. Because if you don't know that it hurts your foot, and my foot hurts, yours don't, you cannot understand how my foot hurts. They cannot understand what it was, how people survived. Every one that survived, survived in a different ways, to be able to talk about it. Some people can't talk about it. Just impossible. I think about it many a times. The world should know. In another 10, 15 years, there's not going to be anyone of us left to even talk about it, because the youngest one today is 70 years old. Because there couldn't be any younger ones, because they didn't take anybody into-- into the working camps, to be able to survive, unless you were 18, 19 years old. So figure it out. Over the years, it's 50 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I'm 69 now. If God will give me another 10 years to live, I'll be thankful. I'd like to live to see my grandchildren get married. And they should have children. I mean, a little [YIDDISH], a little-- a little pleasure to repay for my pains and sufferings. Because nothing in the world will take the place of the little pleasure that you can have from your own children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were blessed that we had two children. My wife couldn't-- couldn't conceive more. She had lost a few times, because her system was so weak, that we tried to have children. So between my daughter and my son, she lost three babies. I would have been the happiest person today, if I could say I have five children, that these five children will start all over again new generations.

speaker: interviewer
text: From your two grandchildren-- from your two children, how many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter has three daughters. And my son has three daughters and two sons. My oldest by my daughter is 22. And by my son, the oldest is 17. So this is my pleasure, that I would like-- and I pray to God every day-- to live to see [YIDDISH] by my children and by my grandchildren. So far, they're showing a good-- good rewards, because they're very good kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Very nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: I really have to be thankful that they are trying not to pain me, hurt me. Like my daughter always used to say, Tata, we have to be different. Because you suffered so much, we cannot do any harm or any hurt. And it's true. They were different children. They were better children than on average. They never gave us any sufferings, any pains. They were the best children in the world. And they are trying to do the same for their children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was married 48 [SIGHS] years-- 48 years and five days. Unfortunately, my wife got sick a year and a half ago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we celebrated our 48th anniversary in the hospital. She hugged me, kissed me for a half an hour. Didn't want to let me go. She felt, [SOBBING] this is going to the last one. She just knew it. And the children were there all the time. My grandchildren were there 24 hours. We didn't know that this was going to be the last. But she was so powerful. She was so strong. She had such a lust for life that she would do anything that they asked her-- chemotherapy, radiation, whatever was asked of her. She did not refuse, because she wanted to live. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING] I know it's very hard to talk about it. I get very emotional about this. You live with someone for 48 years, it's very difficult. She was a very good woman. We worked together. We slept together. We ate together. We suffered together. We were in pain together. We had good times and bad times. And it's difficult.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know, [SIGHS] it's almost a year since she passed away. And I'm trying to put my life together, to be able to continue. Like my daughter said the other day, she said, Tata, you're the only one now. You have to be for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: We need you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] And my grandson always tries to come. My oldest one lives here in the city. He tries to come and spend a few days. He was here. He came Friday. He was here Saturday, Sunday. He stayed till this morning. This morning, he went to school from here. He left me note there. Said he had a wonderful time. I love you. [CRYING] It's very difficult to talk about things like this. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Here, we were planning to enjoy life. We worked for 45 years in the United States, and we were looking forward for good times, to travel all over, to see the world. Unfortunately, it's not what you're planning, it's what God upstairs wants. He does. You can plan till tomorrow. Like they say in Yiddish, [SPEAKING YIDDISH]. That means, you think about it, but He does what He wants, what He pleases.

speaker: interviewer
text: So [SIGHS] what shall I tell you? Thank you very much, Mr. Deutsch.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we have some photos to show.

speaker: interviewer
text: We have some photos [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you can-- I mean, you know-- This is my father. His name is Jacob Deutsch. This picture was taken in 1944. He needed it for a photo. Because those days, you couldn't travel in our country, unless you had to have a passport-- like a passport. On every piece of paper, you had to have a picture. So he made this photo. And we enlarged it. That's what I found when I got home in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this picture was taken in Seghet Maramures, Romania. This family portrait was taken-- I think it was either 1940 or '41, because my father wasn't in-- in our city. He was living in Constanta, next to Bucharest. That's where he was trying to make a living. And he used to come home only once a year. And I remember my mother saying, we have to take a picture, all of us together, and send to my father to see what are-- what their kids look like.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I remember the day that we all dressed up, and we went to this photographer's place. And as you can see, my mother, she was still a young woman. She had no wrinkles on her face.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there's my brothers and sisters, we were all 11 of us. The first one on the left-hand side is my brother, Meyer Isaac. The next one is my older sister, the first one. Her name is Yehudit. And next to my mother is my second-oldest brother. He name is Moshe. He survived in Israel. And my fourth one in the corner, on my right-hand side, is my brother, Leizer Dov. In the second row is my sister, Idele, and my sister, Hindi. Then comes my mother. And the little girl that's in my mother's hand, that was the youngest one. Her name was Perela. Then comes my brother, Simcha. And then, I am in the right-hand corner with the white-- white suit. And the lower left-hand side, in the third row down, is my sister, Risi. And then, is my brother, Sruli.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when we went home in 1946, I went to the same photographer. And I asked him, do you have maybe some photos that you had made in the last 5, 10 years? That's how we found this photograph and my father's picture.

speaker: interviewee
text: Usually, people don't have any-- any kind of photographs, any kind of memories of what their families looked like before the war. So I happened to be fortunate that I remembered the place where we took the picture. And to my luck, I found this picture. It's-- we treasure it very much. My son will not let this picture out of his hand for no money in the world, because that's all he has-- [SOBBING] memory of my mother, my brothers, and sisters. As you see at this picture-- also was a lucky thing-- that is my mother's father, my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my-- the middle one. And he was a merchant. And the two men on the sides were his two sons. It's a story about my grandfather. There was an American journalist came to Romania, to our city. And he took a picture of my grandfather sitting in the front of the store reading a book, a sefer. So he took a picture of him. And when he came back to the United States, he put it into a paper. It's called That paper doesn't exist today. That was about 50, 60 years ago. It was one of the most popular Jewish papers. And he showed a picture there of my grandfather. This is how a merchant in Romania looks like. He's sitting in the front of his store. [LAUGHING] And when you think about it, it was interesting. People that knew my grandfather, rather than the newspaper, and sent back this newspaper to our hometown, [LAUGHS] to show that someone from the United States came there, took a picture of my grandfather, to show what-- [LAUGHING] what it looked like. It-- it was an unbelievable thing. I'll never forget it. And that paper kept on going from house to house. Everybody was reading that article about my grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lucky-- fortunate enough to find this picture. He was-- he was going from his store, going home. And also, an American journalist took this picture, how three Romanian merchants looked like, walking together, hand-in-hand. Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was done in-- in our city, in Sighet Maramures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Approximately when?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was done in-- in 1942.
